text: This meeting is being recorded.
How are you?
Hey, Andy.
I'm
Good.
How are you?
Good.
Where are you calling from?
Vancouver, BC.
Oh, amazing.
That is where Bridget, who is our VP of Ops, lives.
Oh, nice.
Yeah, I think we've got a couple of team members in Vancouver, actually.
So.
Nice.
Well, to be specific, I just moved out last week, so I'm in Maple Ridge.
So like 20 minutes away from Vancouver.
Ah, okay.
Well.
So.
Closer than I am.
I'm in Rhode Island, so this is my day.
Cool.
Well, I know I joined a little bit late.
Sorry about that.
We can start pretty standard interview schedule.
I can give you a sense of how I'm thinking about the role in the context of GrowthX, talk about you, and then we can try and leave 10 minutes at the end for you to ask me questions.
Yeah, perfect.
Perfect.
So.
So I lead the delivery org for growthx.
And we are operating model is sort of like an agency services model today.
So we're delivering like organic blog content, really, so no like video or social or anything like that, no paid ads.
And what we use to generate all of our content is our own internally built AI platform.
So we're running AI workflows on the back end.
Did you talk to Panzer already?
Yes, I did.
you get some of the background?
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Okay, that's fine.
Great.
So we don't have to talk about the platform, but the way that the pods are set up is that we we have a content manager who content managers manage like one to three clients.
And they're generating the content using Atlas.
And then managing editors work with a couple of content managers managing three to five clients.
And you're essentially project managers, also like literal editors.
So you're editing content, kind of the check before it goes out, making sure everything looks good, all the metadata, everything, you know, keywords, alignment, things like that.
So project managing, editorial, so there's some client-facing work there.

And then the other piece is really the ability to kind of express what makes good content so that you can help inform kind of the prompts and pieces that go into building the workflows on the back end.
So those are the different aspects of the managing editor.
what of there.
So can grow.
are a into the process.
partners they

A director or account strategist who is leading two to three pods and they're looking kind of like long-term strategy on the account thinking high level about, you know, the life cycle of a client relationship.

So you're kind of the midpoint, really the core of the project management and making sure that we're on track and then thinking about kind of how the, what we're delivering is helping inform and build the platform and product that we're working on on the back end.
Okay.
Yeah, I get that.
I just have one question.
Yeah.
Is the account strategist responsible for setting up the content strategy throughout or how much of that falls on the managing editor?
So I ask not just about the content deliverables, but also there will be opportunities, say, for, um, protect from...
you Let's� Let's�
Upselling for bottom of the funnel, magnets, for instance, does that fall on the content strategist, or is it more the technical managing editor's role?
Yeah, so the strategist or account director will be doing that, like really own that work.
But depending on the kind of pod makeup, I think we see like managing editors taking on a lot of it, depending on their capacity.
It just, I think we don't have a hard and fast rule about it today.
It is a little bit of a combined effort.
All right.
Awesome.
So why don't you give me your elevator pitch background story?
Yeah.
So I started in content back in high school.
was doing my A-levels in the UK.
So I started working as a content writer.
After 2015, I joined this company called Scale Math as a freelancer, became full-time by 2017.-time by started

I was an individual contributor at the time, primarily B2B SaaS facing, and then I joined this company called Cloudways, I started as a senior executive of content, and then by the end, I was leading the content teams, the team lead, my role was, so we primarily focused on three personas, e-commerce, agencies, and developers.

So, I was responsible for setting, and by the end, I was almost the last one and a half year, I was responsible for managing the content team, creating this content engine, again, well before AI workflows and systems came into play.
So, the aim was to, first of all, define the ICPs, and then deliver content regularly, what people were searching for.
So, SEO plus content, I was also writing, and delivering, and then by the end, I was just editing, and then making sure the content was going out regularly.
And then I joined VPN Overview, this was a subsidiary of Exploit, it's a company based in the Netherlands.
I joined as a senior editor, and by
At end, was a content and SEO coordinator.
Again, the role was originally a senior editor, being very particular about the audiences that we were writing for, and then editing the content accordingly.
We used to hold regular workshops as well, at least two or three a quarter, just to guide writers about common mistakes they were making.
And then by the end, it was a combination of SEO and content.
So guiding writers about SEO best practices, seeing what's working in the industry.
And again, this is just like early COVID, so a lot of change was happening in Google.
Making sure that writers were up to speed around that was critically important.
Then I moved to Canada, and then I joined this company called Alexa Translations, as a senior content writer.
So I was more of like, I was the only person on the team.
So doing the research, then presenting it to the CMO at the time, and then getting by and then writing the content end-to-end production.
That was me.
By the end, I got an offer from...
from...
I joined Directive, Directive is a B2B SaaS agency.
had five clients.
I was managing, sorry, five clients.
I was a part of a pod, so we had SEO specialists under me, and then I was the SEO strategist, and then I reported to an account director.
So all five clients were different industries, cybersecurity, deepfake detection, and then others like finance.
So the role was setting up an SEO strategy and guiding their content team.
So I was like 13th man on their content teams, regular stand-ins with the teams, making sure that deliverables were on point.
Like I was coordinating with their teams, as well as ours internally.
Identifying upsell opportunities was part of that.
Building client relationships, critically important.
We even visited clients for QBRs.
Yeah, so that was that Directive, and then I joined COM100.
It's a local Vancouver-based company as a content marketing manager.
But now is to not edit the content, determine the content strategy for the quarter, and then for the half year, and then lead it end-to-end.
So I'm responsible for the metrics.
Yeah, sorry.
Yeah, great.
So what, I mean, what are you looking for next?
It's been about a year at COM 100.
So, you know, what excites you about GrowthX?
And, you know, what are you looking to do?
So one of the things that really attracted me for this role was, so all my life, if you look at, take a look at my CV, it's a live and breathe content.
And GrowthX, like you originally started off, is like, this is what you do.
And then the intersection of AI-powered workflows, what Panzer, you know, like Panzer, like you called him, showed me, was really impressive.
And then I feel now is the time for me to make the jump because, A, first of all, coming from directive, one of the things that I really miss is the opportunity to learn, right?
Like when you're managing multiple clients.
You just get to learn a lot more.
You run into obstacles, you run into problems, and you figure out how to solve them really quickly.
And at this stage in my career, I think I can be definitely somebody who can take that on.
That's a challenge that I really, really miss.
And then just looking at the description, so I've managed systems, I've managed teams, I've built and scaled content teams, I think could be a great fit for me.

Long term, I see myself somewhere as like the director of organic growth, chief content officer, type of like that, because end-to-end content management, that's something I've done throughout my career.
Awesome.
You mentioned that you've like built out teams and are really like versed in systems thinking.
Can you talk to me about like the process of building out a team and kind of, you know, what you took into account?
Over time, what you had to keep in mind.
Not that this is a management position, but I think like would be curious to learn a little bit more about your systems thinking and how you think about scalability.
Just for context, something that's top of mind for us in general is like building leverage for every new hire.
Right now we're growing linearly.
We're adding clients.
We're adding team members.
But we'd love to be able to build leverage across the team where we're getting efficiency as we're gaining new team members.
Yeah, absolutely.
So I can talk about, so my first experience in building teams was at Cloudways.
So I can start from there.
When I originally joined, we were like, I had nobody under me.
I was just a senior executive of content.

And then we realized that we need to be more focused around the ICPs that we were targeting because e-commerce and agencies were very specific ICPs and they brought in the chunk of revenue for the team.
So what we did was we brought on.
And two hires for each, writers, and then I was the one doing the SEO and the content strategy.
The aim was for each member of the team to be very clear about the metrics that they were going to be reporting on, and then making sure that there was some element of knowledge sharing.
So every week, we would have meetings just to discuss what I had been discovering in the SEO space and any challenges that they were running into.
I prefer proactive communication quite a bit.
So if there is a challenge, I prefer to be getting to know about it early on as compared to right at the end.
This was also the case at Directive.
So I had, when I joined, we had an SEO specialist, we had a pod of SEO specialists that were reporting to me.
And when you're managing five clients, there's always something that can come up.
Two of the team members were in Mexico, right?
So there was also like some overlap that we had to manage in terms of times.
And then one of them, one of the clients actually was in the UK.
Okay, so making sure that deliverables were aligned.
And then expectations were communicated early on was critically important.
So in terms of just managing the team, what we do is we start regular standups in the morning.
And then by the end of the week, we have what I prefer to call like a decompress session, just to talk about anything that you've learned, any issues that you ran into, how you resolve them.
This also gave like team members a chance to be heard, especially like junior members that were brought on.
So yeah, that's essentially my experience.
Right now, we just brought on an internet call 100.
So it's too early for me to speak about really fleshing out the team there.
But yeah.
Yeah, awesome.
Talk to me a little bit about your AI experience, the AI and automation tools you've used in your work.
Yeah, absolutely.
So right now, my tech stack essentially is SEMrush.
And then this is the AI component of SEMrush.
I use Profound, Scrunched.
For managing brand mentions, and just making sure that I have visibility into how the brand is performing.
I use Phrase for competitive analysis.
And then there's this tool called Surfer SEO.
You probably know about it just for LSI keyword research.
And then I've trained my own custom bot on the company's brand voice and guidelines on Claude.
This is essentially the tech stack that I have right now.
I also have access to ChatGPT.
So I essentially, when I'm producing content, it's a mix of both.
And then all the research comes from SEMrush, Profound, and Scratched.
Talk to me a little bit more about like training the bot and Claude about your company's voice.
How do you keep it updated?
What kind of feedback?
Where do you feel like it shines?
And where do you feel like it really doesn't work?
Yeah, absolutely.
So it's very similar to, say, the assignment that I was given by Marilia.
So what I did is I fed it to ICP that we want to target.
Brand voice guidelines, I fed it the kind of content that we don't want, the kind of content that we want.
one of the challenges that I ran into was, I tell you, it kept making repetitive errors, and I essentially dug into why it was making those errors.
And one of its responses was, I thought the original instructions were more suggestive than concrete.
So making sure, I see you probably have run into something like that.
So making sure that wasn't happening.
So this was a part of the refining process, is just checking the content regularly.
And then what worked is, top of the final content, it does really well, right?
But when you're talking to a specific audience, and you want it to be really analytical or technical in terms of what it's putting out, it often lacks there.
So I'm still in the process of fine-tuning the bot for specific ICPs.
And yeah, we ran into that.
A common, one of the things I'll tell you is, so whenever I wanted to reference specific information from the web, it's just how LLM search the web, right?
They look for what's more relevant, and they just put it up.
It's not like how Google search works.
So what it often does is it seeks articles online that are written by our competitors, and you don't want that.
So that is, it's such a common error that keeps popping up now and then, making sure that we stand that out.
But it still, has still been a challenge for me.
But at that point, you already know where this data is coming from.
So you can obviously remove it and then find the actual study that's being referenced.
So for instance, if you're, what it does is it'll give you an answer to like the top 10 AI customer service software that was written by Zendesk.
But the stat within that article was actually referenced by another study done by, say, Cranview Research.
Except it won't reference Cranview Research, it would reference Zendesk.
You don't want.
And that happening.
Right.
So yeah, that's, that's been a challenge.
Yeah, totally.
Okay.
Can you talk me through a time when you've had to quickly shift content strategy for a client?
Absolutely.
This I ran into last year with director.
So part of my role was making sure that they get top of funnel traffic, but also conversions.
And one of the issues that we ran into was they had a director of, like a director that was managing the entire site content.
One of their issues was, why are we doing top of the funnel content?
This isn't going to, like the people that we want to target, they're not going to be searching for what is procure to pay or what is spend automation.
So first of all, we, so what I did was, because again, you have to make sure that all of your clients are satisfied.
What we did was a bottom up approach, started by focusing on alternative pieces, right?
They didn't have any, and they were competing with big names like SAP.
So.

So making sure, so what we did was, we started by creating alternatives pieces, focusing on Airbase, that was the client's name, Airbase versus X competitor, Y competitor, and then build the strategy from the ground up.
So, and then, for instance, we searched for bigger names, and then looked for keyword volume around, say, alternatives.
So SAP alternatives, SAP concur alternatives, ramp alternatives.
We found decent keyword volume around there.
This was a 12-month engagement.
We were in the first three to four months, around when the Darcy, that was her name, and she came and she was like, yeah, this isn't, we don't want to do this.
So what we did is we flipped the strategy on its head.
And again, their core NSM, the North Star metric they were tracking was conversions.
We saw 147% increase in conversions in the next two or three months.
And last, I remember, they definitely, they did renew the contract too, so.
Yeah.
So, yeah.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, those are all the questions I had for you.
What questions do you have for me?
Yeah.
So how does the reporting structure work?
That's the first question I have.
So you mentioned you probably, you gave me a pretty good idea, but I'm just curious, know, I report to the account director and then I'm assuming they have quarterly CSATs going out to the clients.
Um, not yet.
That's, that's on my roadmap.
Um, but yeah, I mean, right now, actually our contracts are, are a lot of our contracts are quarterly.
Um, so it's almost like a, to CSAT in and of itself, right?
They can, they can churn every 90 days.
We're moving to annual, like as we're going through clients.
Um, but, uh, yeah, CSAT is a cue for a goal.
Um, reporting structure, um, is yeah, you'll report to the directors.
The content managers actually report to Panzer.
So you're not direct.
You're directly managing anybody, but you're working very closely with them.
That's really about like a quality rubric because of Panzer's role.
And then, yeah, and then the directors report to me.
Okay.
All right.
That makes sense.
And then one of the other questions I had is generally, so, okay, now I'm curious to ask, what's the retention rate like right now?
For clients or for employees?
Yeah, well, we had a, we're actually refining our ICP right now.
We signed a ton of clients, I think between like February and April.
And at that point, we also scaled up hiring really, really fast.
So it's kind of a, it's a related question for clients and employees, actually.
Um, so we scaled hiring really, really fast.
There was almost no onboarding.
Today, onboarding is like a week and a half.
So it's still pretty short.
But there was we literally like threw everybody into the fire clients and our new team.
And so we've refined both our onboarding practice and and our for clients and for team members since then.
So our team onboarding or our client onboarding now, they go through an eight week strategy sprint before they're handed off to like a long term engagement with the team.
So we refine their artifacts very much what you built out in the context brief.
We do calibration with them.
We build like the full strategy for at least I mean, we kind of shift strategies are always evolving, right?
So like for at least the next like three to four months, kind of this is where we're going.
This is these are the keywords we're targeting.
These are the areas of opportunity.
And then we deliver two to three weeks of ongoing content so that we can continue to calibrate, make sure we're on the right track, and then hand clients off to long-term management.
So we're seeing really good results with that.
I think we've only had one client that hasn't converted from that, and that's been going on for five months, and we've been onboarding two clients a week for five months.
So we've got really good retention on that front.
So I think we learned a lot by taking on clients too quickly without a process in the spring, and we've really refined that.
And we're also, like, we're saying no.
Our sales guy just, like, said no to a client.
And they like emailed Marcel all mad and he had to be like, No, you're like not a good fit for us.
So like people are knocking down the door to work with us.
And we want to make sure that it's like, our delivery is also good.
Like we have, we're only onboarding two clients a week going forward.
Because we don't want to miss the bar.
That is amazing to hear.
Okay, one last question.
From your perspective, what does success look like in this role?
Like what is the key metric that you would want a managing editor to deliver on?
Yeah, I think it's like proactivity, and like interest in improving process.
So that's kind of why I asked you a little bit about like systems thinking and scaling.
As I said, we're trying to build more leverage, because we don't want to onboard two clients a week forever.
So like, the

The thing I mentioned at the very beginning about the ability to really break down content into what makes it good from a technical standpoint, that's what's going to inform the improvement of the workflows and platform at the end of the day.
And the engineers don't know what makes something good.
So we've written prompts, we've refined prompts, and we're constantly working on that.
But the output, like you said, sometimes it's still going to reference a competitor or something like that.
And so we have to continue to find instances of that, point them out, and like, try to help identify patterns for how we can improve the workflows.
And, you know, there, we have MEs that have a ton of like, really deep editorial experience that like, are just like, I'm going to rip this out of the AI, and I'm going to just take a pen.
Um, and so I think like, uh, ME, who's going to succeed long term.
I think like, uh,
That's why we have that technical title at the front, is somebody who's interested in like building leverage for themselves through like the product capabilities that we have on offer.
Yeah.
No, I hear you.
That was one of the challenges I faced as well, just when I started building out an internal prompt library.
A lot of it was just like, oh, should I just go in and fix it?
But especially if you want to build scalable teams.

I mean, another thing is when GPT rolled out the agent mode, one of the things that I did was internally with the teams, we did this workshop where I talked to them about how you can use agent mode in general.
So, yeah, we've seen some efficiency gains there.
And again, I totally hear you when you say like, it's instinctive to just go in and try and fix stuff yourself.
Yeah.
You can now deploy agents that can do part of it for you.
So, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, totally.
Okay.
Anything else?
That's all I have.
All
All right.
Well, Naj, it was great to meet you.
And I will talk to you soon.
Perfect.
Thank you.
Bye-bye.
Bye.
